paper.title: Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: CODS-2025 execution track shows public→hidden Spearman ρ=−0.13 (n=13, p=0.71), statistically indistinguishable from zero. The core assumption that leaderboard rankings transfer to held-out settings is violated.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The assumption that aggregate in-sample scores predict out-of-sample rank ordering. The paper's falsification conditions set ρ<0.85 across ≥2 OOD criteria as threshold for the position being well-supported.
seeds.research_question: Under what conditions (if any) does the predictive validity ρ between in-sample and out-of-sample rank exceed 0.85 for LLM agent configurations?
seeds.hypothesis: PV rank correlation remains below 0.85 across all three OOD criteria (held-out, cross-subset, adversarial) for agentic benchmarks, confirming that aggregate score rankings systematically underspecify deployment evaluation.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Run the proposed pre-registered pilot: 80 configurations, 120 scenarios, ~1,200 perturbed observations. Measure Spearman ρ between in-sample ranking and each OOD criterion ranking. Check against falsification threshold ρ=0.85.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If ρ > 0.95 across all architectures and OOD criteria, the central claim needs downward revision: AssetOpsBench rankings may be highly stable at i.i.d. holdout, and the position may not generalize.
seeds.killer_objection: The paper itself acknowledges the execution-track result is 'suggestive only' with n=13; the CI spans [−0.64, +0.45]. A well-powered positive result (ρ>0.85) would refute the position's central concern.
seeds.killer_result: If the pilot study shows PV rank Jaccard overlap > 0.85 with mean rank (falsification condition F4 fails), the entire proposal offers no different deployment guidance and is operationally moot.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Convergence Pattern A: Four independent teams (G5, G14, G16, G27) localize the same bottleneck to FMSR/TSFM. G14 finds 35.5% of baseline trajectories contain unnecessary expensive tool calls, 96% of them on wrong-domain scenarios.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: No adaptive routing mechanism exists in current agent frameworks. Groups G3, G9, G14, G16, G21 all independently propose a learned meta-router as the missing first-class abstraction.
seeds.research_question: Can a learned meta-router (DistilBERT-scale, ~66M parameters) that routes between cheap/specialist and expensive/generalist tool paths match or exceed the accuracy of always-using-the-most-expensive-path while reducing cost by ≥3×?
seeds.hypothesis: A confidence-gated meta-router trained on domain-aware features can achieve ≥90% tool-selection accuracy and ≥3× cost reduction without significant quality degradation compared to the expensive-only baseline.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Build on G14's confidence-gating approach: (1) collect 500+ AssetOpsBench trajectories with per-tool success/failure labels, (2) train a lightweight binary classifier per tool domain, (3) sweep routing threshold θ∈[0.5,0.95], (4) measure composite cost-quality Pareto frontier.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If meta-router accuracy < 80% or cost reduction < 2× while maintaining quality, then static routing (or simpler heuristics like G14's threshold-based gate) may be sufficient for industrial agent deployment.
seeds.killer_objection: Learned meta-routers add training data dependencies and deployment complexity that may not be justified if simple static heuristic routing (e.g., G14's threshold θ=0.8) already achieves most of the gain.
seeds.killer_result: A meta-router achieving 95% routing accuracy with 5× cost reduction and zero quality degradation would be direct evidence for making it a first-class framework abstraction as the paper recommends.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: PHMForge (G30): LLM-as-judge inter-rater reliability Krippendorff α=0.61 vs human-human α∈[0.74,0.82]. ARE/Gaia2 verifier achieves 0.99 precision/0.95 recall without any LLM judge. Condition Insight: CAR 0.68→0.91 under prompt change.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Current leaderboards implicitly assume LLM-as-judge scores are good proxies for human expert evaluation. The evidence shows the judge is weaker than the experts it surrogates, and score changes from prompt engineering dwarf model choice effects.
seeds.research_question: Can a shared community-maintained rule pipeline (analogous to ARE's DAG oracle) replace LLM-as-judge for at least 80% of trajectory evaluation in a standard agent benchmark, with deterministic precision/recall guarantees?
seeds.hypothesis: A rule-based deterministic verifier can achieve ≥0.95 precision/recall on tool-call hygiene (T2), planning DAG correctness (T3), and evidence grounding (T12) — covering enough of the twelve tiers to serve as an evaluation anchor that all LLM-as-judge scores can be calibrated against.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: (1) Design oracle DAG specifications for 50 AssetOpsBench scenarios, (2) implement rule-based verifier for T2 (tool hygiene), T3 (planning DAG), T12 (evidence grounding), (3) verify against 200 human-labeled trajectories, (4) compare LLM-judge α with and without calibration against rule pipeline.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If rule-based verifier coverage is < 60% of tiers or precision < 0.90, then the judge-independent anchor may need hybrid approaches (LLM+rule) or may be infeasible for open-ended agent trajectories.
seeds.killer_objection: Rule-based oracles require expensive human annotation for oracle DAGs per scenario, which doesn't scale to the thousands-of-scenarios vision the paper advocates.
seeds.killer_result: Demonstrating that a shared rule pipeline can automatically cover T2, T3, and T12 for 500+ scenarios with >0.95 precision (achieving what ARE did at 450-trajectory scale) would eliminate the primary objection to judge-independent benchmarking.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: The paper's Limitations section explicitly states: 'Empirical validation is future work.' Tier independence is 'asserted, not tested.' No data linking framework rankings to real deployed-system outcomes.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The paper argues the twelve tiers are roughly orthogonal and non-redundant, but has not empirically tested this claim. The entire PV score depends on this assumption being approximately correct.
seeds.research_question: What is the empirical dimensionality of the twelve-tier measurement space on a large-scale evaluation data (say, 200+ configurations × 500+ scenarios)?
seeds.hypothesis: Principal Component Analysis of the 12-tier × N-configuration matrix will yield 8-12 significant components (eigenvalue > 1), supporting the claim of non-redundancy. However, T4 (Capability Axes) and T3 (Planning Quality) may show substantial overlap.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: (1) Run 50+ configurations across all twelve tiers on 200+ AssetOpsBench scenarios, (2) compute 12×50 configuration×tier score matrix, (3) PCA to measure effective dimensionality, (4) Spearman rank correlation matrix between tiers to quantify pairwise redundancy.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If PCA shows < 6 significant components, the twelve-tier apparatus is over-parameterized and can be compressed. If > 12 components, there are additional unmeasured dimensions (making the problem harder, not easier).
seeds.killer_objection: The paper's defense that tier-orthogonality is a 'working hypothesis' is genuine but the PV score and falsification design both depend on it. Without empirical validation, the entire framework is a speculation.
seeds.killer_result: Finding that the twelve tiers reduce to < 6 independent dimensions would simplify the evaluation problem enormously — a single aggregate score might after all capture most variance, contradicting the paper's central position.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: All evidence is drawn from industrial asset operations and maintenance (AssetOpsBench). The paper explicitly acknowledges: 'Whether the twelve-tier apparatus generalizes to other MCP-based domains is open.'
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The twelve-tier apparatus, PV score, and OOD criteria are being proposed as general frameworks but have been tested in exactly one domain. Domain-specific factors (e.g., the prominence of MCP-stdio overhead) may not transfer.
seeds.research_question: Does the twelve-tier measurement apparatus and the predictive-validity ranking criterion transfer from industrial asset O&M to at least two other MCP-based agent domains (e.g., scientific code generation, customer service, or enterprise workflow)?
seeds.hypothesis: The twelve tiers will show different coverage patterns across domains: T1-T7 (core capability) will largely transfer, while T8-T12 (deployment extension) will require domain-specific metric adaptations. However, the PV ranking criterion will show ρ<0.85 across all domains tested.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: (1) Replicate the fourteen-study convergent analysis on an MCP-based scientific assistant benchmark (e.g., scientific code generation with tool use), (2) identify how many of the twelve tiers are naturally covered, (3) measure public→hidden rank correlation, (4) test the PV ranking criterion.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If a second domain shows ρ>0.85 for in-sample to OOD rank correlation, the rank-instability problem may be domain-specific rather than structural, and the paper's critique may not generalize.
seeds.killer_objection: Domain-specificity is the strongest limitation. Without cross-domain evidence, the paper is essentially an improved evaluation framework for industrial asset O&M agents — valuable but not the field-wide shift it advocates.
seeds.killer_result: A second domain showing ρ>0.90 public→hidden rank correlation would substantially weaken the paper's argument that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify evaluation.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: QLoRA study (G20): Weight-internalizing tool knowledge eliminates 82.6% of input tokens and improves AT-F1 0.65 vs 0.47, but catastrophic forgetting probe shows Gemma retains 79.8% and Qwen3 retains only 61.3% of pre-training MCQ accuracy.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Benchmarks only measure direct task performance, not retention of pre-training knowledge. Weight-modifying optimizations (fine-tuning, QLoRA, adapter layers) can trade off downstream task performance against general capability in ways no current tier captures.
seeds.research_question: How much pre-training knowledge retention is lost per unit of task-specific gain in LLM agent fine-tuning, and should a 'capability retention' sub-metric be added as a mandatory reportable in agent leaderboards?
seeds.hypothesis: There exists a measurable retention-efficiency Pareto frontier where task gains beyond 10-15% AT-F1 improvement correlate with >20% loss in held-out pre-training MCQ accuracy for small LLMs (< 10B parameters).
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: (1) Fine-tune 3 base models (Gemma-4-E4B, Qwen3-4B, Llama-4-Maverick-17B) at 5 adapter ranks each on AssetOpsBench tool catalogs, (2) measure AT-F1 gain and pre-training MCQ retention, (3) compute retention-efficiency slope, (4) validate on a held-out model family.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If retention loss < 5% even at maximum task gain, then catastrophic forgetting may be model-specific (Qwen3) rather than structural, and a retention sub-metric is low priority.
seeds.killer_objection: MCQ accuracy on pre-training benchmarks is itself a proxy; actual deployment capability retention (e.g., reasoning, safety, instruction following across domains) is what matters and is harder to measure.
seeds.killer_result: Demonstrating that every 10% task gain costs >15% general capability retention across all model families tested would establish 'capability retention' as a mandatory tier in agent leaderboards.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Convergence Pattern C: G7 reports 5-6s residual per MCP-stdio call. G12 finds direct in-process tools outperform MCP on both pass and judge mean. G27 measures model-loading 233ms→26ms via pre-loading. The paper's Proposal 4 calls for persistent, non-stdio infra.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Current agent benchmarks systematically overestimate reasoning time because MCP-stdio subprocess spawn and transport overhead is conflated with reasoning latency. Reported latency improvements from 'better reasoning' may partly reflect protocol overhead reductions.
seeds.research_question: What fraction of 'agent thinking time' in current leaderboard benchmarks is attributable to MCP-stdio protocol overhead vs actual model inference and reasoning latency?
seeds.hypothesis: At least 30-50% of end-to-end latency in MCP-based agent benchmarks is MCP-stdio subprocess overhead (spawn, discovery, transport), not reasoning or inference time — meaning headline latency comparisons systematically over-state differences in agent reasoning capability.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Instrument the full AssetOpsBench pipeline with per-call profiler: (1) measure MCP-stdio overhead (spawn + transport + discovery) vs model inference time separately, (2) compare against non-MCP direct-call baseline, (3) compute 'reasoning overhead ratio' = (total − inference)/inference.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If MCP-stdio overhead < 15% of end-to-end latency, then protocol overhead is a minor concern and the paper's Proposal 4 (persistent non-stdio MCP) is much lower priority than other recommendations.
seeds.killer_objection: The paper's own finding that G27 achieved 3.3× speedup through torch.compile + GPU but bf16 regressed 3.3×→2.6× shows that infrastructure optimization is already underway; persistent MCP may soon be standard practice without needing a field recommendation.
seeds.killer_result: Demonstrating that MCP-stdio overhead accounts for ≥50% of reported latency would be direct evidence that current leaderboards systematically misattribute protocol cost to reasoning capability, justifying the paper's call for latency decomposition as a mandatory submission element.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: The paper proposes a pre-registered pilot (80 configurations, 120 scenarios, 1,200 perturbed observations) with a decision rule: if pilot ρ > 0.95, revise claim downward; if 0.65-0.95, proceed; if < 0.65, claim strongly supported.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The pilot design assumes that rank instability in the execution track (ρ=−0.13) generalizes to a controlled multi-architecture study. But the execution track's wide CI [−0.64, +0.45] and small n=13 mean the true effect could range from strongly negative to moderately positive.
seeds.research_question: Does the pre-registered pilot study confirm or refute the position that aggregate-score leaderboard rankings show predictive validity ρ < 0.85 across all three OOD criteria?
seeds.hypothesis: The pilot will find ρ in the (0.65, 0.95) range: the position is partially supported (rankings transfer imperfectly) but the effect is strong enough to warrant the full PV-score framework. Cross-subset (Criterion B) will show the lowest ρ.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Execute the proposed pilot exactly as specified in Section 6: (1) stratified 80/40 train/holdout on 120 AssetOpsBench scenarios, (2) run across 3 architectures × varying configurations, (3) compute Spearman ρ for each OOD criterion, (4) apply decision rule.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If pilot ρ > 0.95, the paper's central claim is refuted for AssetOpsBench i.i.d. holdouts — aggregate scores may be sufficient for within-benchmark ranking, and the paper pivots to methodology contribution only.
seeds.killer_objection: The pilot's power calculation (80 paired ranks distinguish ρ=0.85 vs ρ=0.95 at α=0.01 with power >0.9) doesn't address the more relevant threshold of ρ=0.85 vs ρ=0.00 (the null of no transfer), which would require far fewer samples.
seeds.killer_result: A pilot result showing ρ > 0.95 would be the most impactful negative result for the paper's thesis: it would show that for i.i.d. holdouts, aggregate score rankings actually transfer well, limiting the paper's critique to adversarial (Criterion C) scenarios only.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: All proposed predictive-validity criteria remain internal to AssetOpsBench's measurement apparatus. 'We do not yet have data linking framework rankings to real deployed-system outcomes (operator override rate, incident reduction, false-alarm rate).'
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: The paper assumes that improving predictive validity of benchmark rankings will improve deployment decisions. But there is zero empirical evidence connecting any benchmark ranking (PV or mean) to real-world outcomes like operator satisfaction, maintenance cost reduction, or safety incident rate.
seeds.research_question: What is the relationship between in-benchmark predictive validity scores and real-world deployment outcomes (operator override rate, mean-time-between-failures, false alarm rate, cost savings) in industrial asset O&M agents?
seeds.hypothesis: PV-ranked configurations will show 2× better correlation with real-world outcomes than mean-ranked configurations, but even PV's absolute correlation with deployment outcomes may be modest (< 0.4) due to unmeasured real-world confounders.
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: Industry partnership study: (1) deploy top-3 PV-ranked and top-3 mean-ranked configurations in a live CMMS environment for 30 days, (2) measure: operator override rate, false alarm rate, maintenance request accuracy, (3) compute correlation between benchmark rank and real-world metrics.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If PV rank shows no better correlation with real-world outcomes than mean rank (Δρ < 0.1), the entire predictive-validity framework may be a sophisticated solution to a problem that doesn't exist in practice.
seeds.killer_objection: Real-world deployment metrics are confounded by human operators, organizational processes, and site-specific factors that no benchmark can control for. The paper's entire framework may be optimizing for a proxy that doesn't meaningfully affect real outcomes.
seeds.killer_result: Demonstrating that PV-ranked configurations achieve ≥30% fewer operator overrides than mean-ranked configurations in a controlled 30-day deployment study would provide the strongest possible evidence for the paper's thesis.

seeds.trigger_evidence_summary: Visual inspection extension (G23): AWQ W4A16 with domain-specific calibration is the rare optimization improving both speed (1.99×) and accuracy (pass 0.48→0.82). But FP8 KV-cache + chunked prefill + prefix caching causes 0/44 output collapse on Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Image downscaling to 512px produces latency regression.
seeds.hidden_assumption_or_gap: Multi-modal agent deployments create new failure modes (FP8 KV-cache corruption of visual-token prefixes, resolution knee for fixed-tile encoders) that are invisible to text-only agent benchmarks. The twelve-tier apparatus has no tier for modality-specific serving interactions.
seeds.research_question: Does the twelve-tier measurement apparatus need a T13 for 'Modality-Specific Serving Interaction' that captures quantization×encoder×(serving flag) interactions, or can these be subsumed under existing T8 (Deployment Infrastructure) and T10 (Reasoning Mode)?
seeds.hypothesis: A dedicated multi-modal serving tier is necessary because the failure modes are structurally different: FP8-KV corruption of visual prefixes is not a 'deployment infrastructure' issue (it's encoder-specific) and not a 'reasoning mode' issue (the model doesn't output corrupted text — it outputs nothing).
seeds.minimum_viable_experiment: (1) Design a multi-modal serving sweep testing 32 model×quantization×serving-pipeline combinations on the visual inspection scenarios, (2) measure: output collapse rate, latency by serving flag, accuracy by quantization strategy, (3) factor analyze failure modes to test orthogonality with existing tiers.
seeds.negative_result_interpretation: If all multi-modal failure modes load onto existing tiers T8 and T10 in factor analysis, a dedicated T13 is unnecessary and the visual inspection case is a specific instance of general deployment infrastructure issues.
seeds.killer_objection: The paper's twelve-tier apparatus is already asserted as having 12 approximately-orthogonal dimensions — adding a 13th contradicts the claim that the measurement space has 'roughly twelve' dimensions. The better response is showing the existing tiers subsume multi-modal failures.
seeds.killer_result: Demonstrating that 0/44 complete output collapse is a novel failure mode orthogonal to T1-T12 would directly motivate extending the apparatus to T13 for modality-specific serving interactions.
